name: workspace
context: Manage pnpm monorepos with workspace protocol dependencies, filters, recursive runs, and package graph targeting
info: This skill covers pnpm's workspace-native model: the required `pnpm-workspace.yaml` root file, the `workspace:` protocol that forbids accidental registry fallback, graph selectors used by `--filter`, recursive execution with `-r`, and the special behavior of injected workspace dependencies. It matters because autonomous agents working in pnpm monorepos must scope changes precisely or risk mutating the wrong package set.
triggers: add one workspace package to another
run a command in filtered workspaces
use the workspace protocol in pnpm
find why a package is installed in pnpm
body:
# pnpm Workspace Operations

A pnpm workspace is defined by `pnpm-workspace.yaml`. Once that file exists, filters and the `workspace:` protocol become the safest way to target package changes, script runs, and dependency graph traversal.

```yaml
packages:
  - apps/*
  - packages/*
```

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "@acme/ui": "workspace:*"
  }
}
```

Without `workspace:`, pnpm may fall back to the registry when a local version does not satisfy the declared range. That is often the wrong behavior for a monorepo automation task.

sections.title: The workspace protocol
sections.body: The official workspace docs explain that `workspace:` forces pnpm to resolve only to a local workspace package. If the requested version is not present locally, installation fails instead of silently pulling from the registry. Use `workspace:*` for most internal package links and explicit versions like `workspace:1.2.3` when release coordination matters.

sections.title: Filter selectors
sections.body: Filters are the primary blast-radius control for pnpm monorepo automation. `foo...` includes a package and its dependencies, `...foo` includes its dependents, `{packages/**}` scopes by directory, and `[origin/main]` scopes by changed packages since a ref. Combine them to target exactly the packages that should change before using add, run, exec, or deploy.

sections.title: Recursive commands
sections.body: Use `-r` or `--recursive` when you intentionally want to fan out across a workspace. Pair it with `--filter` to narrow the set and `--parallel` only when topological ordering does not matter. For script and binary execution patterns built on these flags, see [[pnpm/scripts#recursive-execution]].

sections.title: Injected workspace dependencies
sections.body: In `package.json`, `dependenciesMeta.<name>.injected` turns a local workspace dependency into a hard-linked copy in the virtual store instead of a source symlink. This is useful when different consumers need the same local package with different peer environments. The tradeoff is that updates are no longer immediately visible; a rebuild or reinstall is often required to refresh the injected copy.

links.context: Install mutations should use filters or workspace-root flags
Recursive runs and execs build directly on workspace selectors
Deploy requires a package selector in workspace repos

related.note: Filtered installs and adds are workspace operations in practice
